texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Human activities have caused changes in global climate, and emissions of carbon dioxide are believed to be responsible for a warming world [1,2]. According to the International Energy Agency, global carbon dioxide emissions rose by 4.6% in 2010, which is equal to 1.3 GtCO2 [3]. Carbon dioxide emissions are projected to grow at an average annual rate of 1.2% [4] and climate models predict that atmospheric CO2 concentration will reach 540 -970 ppm by 2100 [1]. The increased CO2 concentration in the atmosphere is primarily attributable to the burning of fossil fuels. In 2010, 43% of total carbon dioxide emissions arose from coal [3], and coal will continue to play an indispensable role in the near future because it is inexpensive and still abundant [5]. In the United States, coal accounted for approximately 45% of electricity generation in 2009 [6]. The Energy Information Agency claims that the global consumption of coal will increase by 50% by the year 2030 [4]. To mitigate human-related climate change, carbon capture and sequestration (CCS) is an enabling technology that would reduce carbon dioxide emissions signi fi cantly.

texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Post-combustion carbon dioxide capture from power plant fl ue gas can play an important role in addressing current CO2 emission concerns. Air Liquide is developing a sub-ambient membrane process to remove CO2 from fl ue gas by careful process engineering and heat integration. We have successfully validated enhanced low temperature membrane performance reported by Air Liquide using Matrimid s 5218. Asymmetric hollow fi ber membranes with a CO2/N2 selectivity of 209 and CO2 permeance of 227 GPU at � 50 1 C have been fabricated. The CO2/N2 mixed gas transport properties at � 20 1 C, � 30 1 C, � 40 1 C and � 50 1 C are measured in an effort to better understand fundamentals of the enhanced low temperature performance. The permeance of CO2 is minimally affected by temperature while comparatively large increases in CO2/N2 selectivity are observed. The permeance of CO2 increases with increasing pressure while minimal changes in CO2/N2 selectivity are observed. High productivity and high selectivity are achievable with laboratory spun Matrimid s hollow fi bers. A hypothesis regarding the fi ber's fused-nodular skin morphology is proposed and supported by con fi rmatory experiments.

However, this method is energy intensive and a large increase in the cost of electricity (COE) produced will be resulted if used to scrub CO2 from fl ue gas [2,7 -9]. The goal of the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) is to develop advanced CO2 capture and sequestration technologies that can achieve at least 90% removal of CO2 from fl ue gas with less than 35% increase in the cost of energy produced [10]. Technologies that can be applied to CO2 capture include: pre-combustion, post-combustion, oxycombustion and chemical looping [6]. Membranes are intrinsically energy ef fi cient and are logical for consideration in both precombustion and post-combustion processes.

texts.orig: An advantage of applying membrane technology to postcombustion CO2 capture is the ease of retro fi tting to existing stateof-the-art power plants, allowing current power plants to remain in operation. Currently, there are more than fi ve hundred, 500 MW coalfi red power plants in the United States with an average life of 35 years [5]. Virtually, all of these power plants generate electricity by direct combustion of coal with air in a boiler. Post-combustion CO2 capture can play a key role in addressing their CO2 emission concerns. A postcombustion CO2 capture process based on sub-ambient temperature operation of asymmetric hollow fi ber membranes was developed at Air Liquide [11,12]. A simpli fi ed block diagram of the ' cold membrane process ' is shown in Fig. 1. The pre-treated fl ue gas is compressed and dried before being sent to the Low Temperature Membrane System. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Before permeation tests were carried out, fi bers were potted into mini-modules. A typical mini-module consisted of 10 fi bers with an effective length of 20 cm. Both pure gas and mixed gas permeation tests were performed on a constant pressure system, which is described in previous work [14,15]. Feed was introduced at either bore or shell side of the hollow fi ber module and permeate was collected at the other side. The permeate side was maintained at atmospheric pressure and bubble fl owmeters were used to measure gas permeation rate. A temperature chamber (Benchtop, ESPEC North America, MI) was used to control temperatures accurately from � 70 1 C to 180 1 C.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mixed gas permeation was performed with a mixture of 20 mol % CO2/80 mol% N2. The stage cut (ratio of permeate fl ow rate to feed fl ow rate) was maintained less than 1% to ensure a uniform feed composition. The permeate composition was analyzed by gas chromatography (Bruker 430-GC, Agilent Technologies, CA). Steady state was con fi rmed by multiple measurements of permeation rates and analyses of permeate composition. Due to the swelling effect of CO2 at sub-ambient temperatures, more than 12 h were allowed at the temperatures lower than � 10 1 C to ensure gas transport reached equilibrium.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To characterize sorption of CO2 and N2 in hollow fi ber membranes, a pressure decay method was used. Detailed procedures of pressure decay sorption are well described in literature [16,17]. In this work, to perform sorption at sub-ambient temperatures, the sorption system was placed in a temperature chamber which could provide temperatures as low as � 70 1 C. Pneumatic valves were used in the system and were automatically controlled by a computer. At temperatures lower than � 10 1 C, 1 -2 days were waited at each pressure before sorption results were recorded. Note that N2 isotherms were generated fi rst, followed by CO2

texts.orig: 3.1. Formation of high flux ' nodular-skinned ' hollow fiber membranes
texts.text: 3.1. Formation of high flux ' nodular-skinned ' hollow fiber membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A new set of Matrimid s hollow fi ber membranes with nodular skin morphology has been successfully spun. The newly formed membranes displayed higher permeance than those spun in a previous work [18], as is shown in Table 2. Note that both sets of membranes have to be post-treated with polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) in order to achieve desirable selectivity. The objective of this treatment was to plug pinhole defects in the skin layer and prevent them from providing a nonselective fl ow pathway. As can be seen from Fig. 2, without PDMS caulking, the O2/N2 selectivity and CO2/N2 selectivity were far below the material ' s intrinsic dense film values (the O2/N2 selectivity for Matrimid s dense fi lm at 35 1 C is 6.7 and CO2/N2 selectivity is 36 [13]). However, signi fi cant improvement was achieved after PDMS caulking and the membranes were performing close to expected.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The same ' dry-jet/wet-quench ' spinning process as that used in the previous work [18] was employed, and the same dope composition was chosen: 26.2% Matrimid s , 58.9% NMP and 14.9% EtOH (all are weight percentages). However, some of the spinning parameters are different. Table 3 compares spinning parameters used in the current work and previous work. The quench bath temperature was 2.5 1 C higher in the current work and the air gap was 5 cm shorter. A slightly higher quench bath temperature promotes phase separation, but the effect of 2.5 1 C difference should be minimal. However, a 5 cm difference in air gap is a significant shift, which is presumably the main reason affecting resultant membrane property. Carruthers [19] suggested three possible compositional paths in the air gap: (1) solvent loss due to the evaporation of volatile solvent; (2) non-solvent loss due to the evaporation of volatile non-solvent; and (3) non-solvent gain

texts.orig: due to the infusion of non-solvent (moisture) from the surrounding environment. In the current work, the small vapor pressure of NMP (0.0004 bar at 20 1 C) would likely cause the air gap to be relatively saturated with this solvent, making it hard to evaporate. Therefore, the loss of NMP in the air gap should be minimal. However, the non-solvent EtOH has appreciable volatility (0.0595 bar at 20 1 C), and the high spinneret temperature (50 1 C) promotes its evaporation in the air gap, driving the dope composition at the outer layer of nascent fi ber away from the point of phase separation. On the contrary, the infusion of non-solvent moisture due to environment humidity brings the dope composition close to phase separation. These two compositional paths can be visualized from the ternary phase diagram in Fig. 3. In the air gap, the combinational effects of these two factors determine fi nal dope composition of the skin layer prior to phase separation. A change in the air gap would lead to a change in the dope composition, resulting in a different nodular structure. The higher permeance of the membranes fabricated in the current work indicates a more open structure and the SEM image of the fi ber's skin morphology is shown in Fig. 4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 35 1 C. Although the permeance of CO2 decreased with the decrease of temperature, it started to increase as temperature dropped from � 40 1 C to � 50 1 C. This seems like an experimental anomaly, however, it has been veri fi ed for three times and similar trend can also be found in Hasse's work [12]. This trend contradicts the prediction of the Arrhenius relationship [20], and the reason is attributed to the conditioning of polymer matrix by highly sorbing CO2 molecules. The higher activity of CO2 at � 50 1 C may increase the number of subtle packing disruptions in the non-equilibrium polymer matrix, making it energetically easier for penetrant molecules to be sorbed and to diffuse. The permeance of N2 was also increased, from 0.87 GPU at � 40 1 C to 1.19 GPU at � 50 1 C. Here, activity is de fi ned as the ratio of gas partial pressure to its pure gas vapor pressure [21,22]. For non-ideal gas, pressure is replaced by fugacity. The vapor pressures of CO2 and corresponding fugacities at different temperatures are listed in Table 5. The lower the temperature, the lower the CO2 vapor pressure, and thus the higher its activity for a fi xed partial pressure. As shown later, activity alone is insuf fi cient to explain the observed phenomenon, since shell side at the same feed activity does not show the same results as bore-side feed (Section 3.4). The combination of elevated CO2 activity and bore-side feed are necessary to cause the desirable phenomenon. To clearly illustrate the unexpected impact of low temperature, the natural logarithm of CO2/N2 selectivity and the permeance of CO2 are plotted against the reciprocal of absolute temperature, as is shown in Fig. S1 in the supplemental. A linear fi tting curve extracted from data at high temperatures (35 1 C to � 10 1 C) clearly shows the deviation of CO2 permeance at � 50 1 C.

texts.orig: 3.3. Cold membrane performance
texts.text: 3.3. Cold membrane performance

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The performance of PDMS post-treated hollow fi ber membranes under different feed pressures was investigated at � 20 1 C, � 30 1 C, � 40 1 C and � 50 1 C. The feed was a mixture of 20 mol% CO2/80 mol% N2, and was introduced at bore side of the hollow fi ber module. At each temperature, feed pressure was increased from 3.45 bar gauge to 24 bar gauge, with a stepped increment of 3.45 bar gauge. Merkel [7] pointed out that fl ue gas CO2 capture is pressure-ratio-limited, and in practical separation applications, the pressure ratio (feed pressure to permeate pressure) is usually between 5 and 15. This range is limited by the capital and energy costs of generating the required pressure difference across the membrane. The cold membrane process is highly energy integrated [12]; thus potentially economically allowing the use of relatively high feed pressures (10 -15 bar). In this laboratory study, the feed pressure was varied from 3.45 bar gauge to 24 bar gauge, giving rise to a maximum pressure ratio of 24. Increased selectivity is of clear bene fi t under high pressure ratio operating conditions.

texts.orig: should be minimal. From Fig. 6 we can see that, the highest CO2/N2 selectivity and highest CO2 permeance were achieved at � 50 1 C. With a feed pressure of 10.3 bar gauge, the CO2/N2 selectivity was 209 and CO2 permeance was 227 GPU. Moreover, as feed pressure increases, the permeance of CO2 increases with slight change in CO2/N2 selectivity. Similar trend was observed in polyamide desalination membranes [25], but the formation mechanism and the structure of the selective layer are different from membranes formed in the current work . The phenomenon of increased productivity with minimal change in selectivity is unusual and exciting. A gas separation membrane's selectivity and productivity are typically trade-off parameters, and under normal conditions, the selectivity generally decreases with increasing permeance of the more permeable gas component [26,27]. The reason for the unusual behavior of membranes produced in this work is attributed to the special morphology of the fi ber's selective skin layer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The nodular morphology of the skin layer was described in a previous work [18], but more details are revealed here. Fig. 7 shows the image of a hypothetical nodular structure. The nodules are hypothesized to be of high polymer density, and the interstitial domains between nodules are of low polymer density. Similar structure was also shown in the previous work of Kesting [28] and Dong [29]. They suggested that the smaller displacements between chain segments within the nodules may correspond to the Henry's sorption sites, whereas the larger displacements in the interstitial domains may constitute the Langmuir sorption sites. However, we believe that both Langmuir and Henry's sorption sites exist within the nodules and the interstitial domains, but to different degrees. Since the interstitial domain has higher fractional free volume (FFV), more gas molecules are sorbed to this region than to the surrounding nodules. When feed is applied on the bore side, internal stress in fl ates the fi bers, resulting in slight separation of nodules with orientation of the polymer chains connecting adjacent nodules. The dilated interstitial domain sorbs more gas molecules, resulting in increased permeance and increased sorption selectivity. The oriented polymer chains are size selective, favoring the diffusion of CO2 molecules over N2 molecules, leading to increased diffusion selectivity. So far, no characterization has been done that may provide insight on structural changes of the nodular skin, because the skin layer only accounts for  1% of the fi ber wall and it is hard to separate it from the support layer.

texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The escalating level of CO2 concentration in the atmosphere is generally accepted as a factor causing climate change. Post-combustion CO2 capture offers the advantages of an easy retro fi t to existing state-of-the-art power plants without interrupting power plant operation when CO2 capture encounters dif fi culty. Membranes are compact and energy-ef fi cient separation devices, which makes them a promising technology for post-combustion CO2 capture. Sub-ambient temperature operation provides a mechanism to simultaneously achieve high CO2/N2 selectivity and high permeance. The energy integrated cold membrane process developed by American Air Liquide provides a promising path for cost effective CO2 capture from fl ue gas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, asymmetric Matrimid s hollow fi ber membranes with fused-nodular selective skins were successfully spun. The PDMS post-treatment is necessary to ensure desirable membrane selectivity by caulking pinhole defects in the fi ber's skin layer. Although additional transport resistance is added, the membrane productivity is still much higher than those with defect free dense selective layers. At temperatures lower than � 20 1 C, membrane productivity increased with increasing feed pressure. Only a slight change in CO2/N2 selectivity was observed. This is believed to result from high CO2 activity at low temperatures combined with oriented polymer chains at the inter-nodular zones. The higher CO2/N2 selectivity and higher permeance of CO2 for bore-side feed and increased CO2/N2 diffusion selectivity with reduced temperature supported the hypothesis of nodular skin morphology playing an important role in the enhanced membrane performance of Matrimid s at low temperatures.
